summary: American New York School poet. Best known for Lunch Poems (containing "The Day Lady Died" and "A Step Away from Them"), "Having a Coke with You," and the manifesto "Personism." Worked as a curator at the MoMA. Called a "poet among painters" by biographer Marjorie Perloff.

works.name: The Day Lady Died
works.description: Poem from Lunch Poems mourning the death of Billie Holiday. Set on "12:20 in New York on a Friday / three days after Bastille day."

clues.clue: Set on "12:20 in New York on a Friday / three days after Bastille day" — speaker gets a shoeshine, sees Holiday's face on the New York Post
examples: it is 12:20 in New York on a Friday three days after Bastille day
set 'three days after Bastille day' and the speaker sees the title singer's face on the front page of the New York Post

clues.clue: Ends with "everyone and I stopped breathing" — remembering Holiday performing at the 5 Spot nightclub
examples: everyone and I stopped breathing
watched the title character perform in the 5 Spot nightclub

clues.clue: Bank teller "Stillwagon" — thought to be a veiled reference to a hearse
examples: the surname of the bank teller 'Stillwagon' is thought to be a not-so-veiled reference to a hearse

works.name: Having a Coke with You
works.description: Poem addressed to life partner, dancer Vincent Warren. The title action is "more fun than going to San Sebastian."

clues.clue: Speaker "would rather look at you than all the portraits in the world / except possibly for the Polish Rider" — addressed to dancer Vincent Warren
examples: would rather look at you than all the portraits in the world / except possibly for the Polish Rider
life partner, dancer Vincent Warren, is the addressee

clues.clue: Title action is "more fun than going to San Sebastian" or "being sick to my stomach" in Barcelona — never thinks of Duchamp's Nude Descending a Staircase at home
examples: the title action is 'more fun than going to San Sebastian'
doesn't think of Marcel Duchamp's Nude Descending a Staircase while at home

clues.clue: Addressee's "orange shirt" made them look like a "better, happier St. Sebastian"
examples: the addressee's 'orange shirt' made them look like a 'better, happier St. Sebastian'

works.name: Lunch Poems
works.description: Poetry collection published by City Lights Bookstore's Pocket Poets Series. Written during lunch breaks at MoMA. Contains "The Day Lady Died," "Personal Poem," and "A Step Away from Them."

clues.clue: Published by City Lights Pocket Poets Series — written during lunch breaks at MoMA
examples: published with the help of City Lights
wrote while he was a curator at the MoMA

clues.clue: Contains "A Step Away from Them" — carries papaya juice and copy of Poems by Pierre Reverdy back to work
examples: carries his papaya juice and copy of Poems by Pierre Reverdy back to work
a collection of poems that he wrote during these periods of time

works.name: Why I Am Not a Painter
works.description: Poem about the relationship between poetry and painting. Depicts Mike Goldberg painting "SARDINES" (starting with "ORANGES").

clues.clue: Depicts Mike Goldberg painting SARDINES — speaker notes "how terrible orange is / and life"
examples: depicted Mike Goldberg painting Sardines in 'Why I am Not a Painter'
a creator of this art form notes 'how terrible orange is / and life'

works.name: Personism: A Manifesto
works.description: Tongue-in-cheek 1959 manifesto proposing to put poems "between two persons rather than two pages."

clues.clue: Asserts poems should be "between two persons rather than two pages" — born from realization he could "use the telephone instead"
examples: putting poems 'between two persons rather than two pages,' called 'Personism'
his realization, while writing a poem for someone he was in love with, that 'if I wanted to I could use the telephone instead'

clues.clue: "Only Whitman and Crane and Williams... are better than the movies"
examples: asserts that 'only Whitman and Crane and Williams ... are better than the movies'

clues.clue: Born after spending a lunch period with LeRoi Jones (Amiri Baraka)
examples: Personism was born after its author spent one of these periods of time with LeRoi Jones

works.name: Lana Turner has collapsed!
works.description: Poem mocking tabloid headlines, telling the title sex symbol "we love you get up."

clues.clue: Tells the title actress "we love you get up" after learning she "has collapsed!" — inspired by newspaper read on Staten Island ferry
examples: tells the title sex symbol, 'we love you get up'
describes braving poor weather before seeing the phrase 'LANA TURNER HAS COLLAPSED!'

clues.clue: Declares "there is no snow in Hollywood"
examples: declaring that 'there is no snow in Hollywood'

works.name: To the Film Industry in Crisis
works.description: Poem proclaiming "Motion Picture Industry, / it's you I love!"

clues.clue: Proclaims "Motion Picture Industry, / it's you I love!" — taught by "stretching Vistavision" and "tragic Technicolor"
examples: proclaimed 'Motion Picture Industry, / it's you I love!'

works.name: Ave Maria
works.description: Poem asking the "mothers of America" to "let your kids go to the movies!"

clues.clue: Opens by asking the "mothers of America" to "let your kids go to the movies!"
examples: opens by asking the 'mothers of America' to 'let your kids go to the movies!'

works.name: Mayakovsky
works.description: Poem noting "I am quietly waiting for the catastrophe of my personality to seem beautiful again."

clues.clue: "I am quietly waiting for the catastrophe of my personality to seem beautiful again" — exclaims "That's funny! there's blood on my chest"

clues.clue: Wrote series of eulogies for James Dean — accused of "necrophilia" by Life magazine reader

comprehensive_summary:
Frank O'Hara is an American New York School poet, alongside John Ashbery, Kenneth Koch, James Schuyler, and Ted Berrigan. He worked as a curator at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) and was labeled a "poet among painters" by biographer Marjorie Perloff.

His most famous collection, Lunch Poems, was published by City Lights Bookstore's Pocket Poets Series and written during lunch breaks at MoMA. It contains "The Day Lady Died," a poem mourning Billie Holiday's death, set on "12:20 in New York on a Friday / three days after Bastille day" where the speaker gets a shoeshine and sees Holiday's face on the New York Post, ending "everyone and I stopped breathing" while remembering her performance at the 5 Spot nightclub.

"Having a Coke with You," addressed to his life partner, dancer Vincent Warren, declares the title action "more fun than going to San Sebastian." The speaker says he "would rather look at you than all the portraits in the world / except possibly for the Polish Rider" and never thinks of Duchamp's Nude Descending a Staircase at home.

"Why I Am Not a Painter" depicts Mike Goldberg painting "SARDINES" and notes "how terrible orange is / and life." His tongue-in-cheek manifesto "Personism" proposes putting poems "between two persons rather than two pages," born from realizing he could "use the telephone instead." It asserts "only Whitman and Crane and Williams... are better than the movies" and was born after a lunch with LeRoi Jones.

Other poems include "Lana Turner has collapsed!" (telling the actress "we love you get up"), "To the Film Industry in Crisis" ("Motion Picture Industry, it's you I love!"), "Ave Maria" (asking mothers to let kids go to the movies), and "Mayakovsky" ("I am quietly waiting for the catastrophe of my personality to seem beautiful again").

O'Hara collaborated with Norman Bluhm on "poem-paintings" and with Larry Rivers on the Stones lithographs. He wrote a 1959 monograph on Jackson Pollock despite enduring Pollock's homophobic abuse, and helped bring Abstract Expressionism to Europe in the 1958 exhibition The New American Painting with Dorothy Miller. He praised close friend Willem de Kooning as Action Painting's "Delacroix" in the poem "Radio."
